mappings.rationaleAI: Stone Age cave paintings at Chauvet and Lascaux, including multicoloured animal depictions and hand outlines, represent the earliest known cave and mural painting (B.5.a.i) and are foundational to the history of non-Western painting (B.6.b).

Traces a single iconographic subject through centuries of Western painting by Caravaggio, Artemisia Gentileschi, and Johann Liss, illustrating how biblical narrative shaped painting iconography (B.2) and the development of devotional and narrative representational painting (B.5.b.i) across Renaissance and Baroque periods (B.6.a).

Morisot's Impressionist innovations in open-air painting technique and compositional choices address design principles (B.1) and painting media (B.3), while her genre and portrait subjects exemplify representational painting (B.5.b.i) within the history of Western painting (B.6.a).

Dürer's watercolour hare and praying hands drawings exemplify drawing by subject matter (A.3.a), while his woodcut rhinoceros and commercial print market represent relief printmaking media (C.2.a) and the history of both drawing (A.4) and printmaking (C.4) during the Northern Renaissance.

Hokusai's Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji were produced as ukiyo-e woodblock prints (C.2.a), incorporating Western pigments into traditional Japanese printmaking. His influence on European Impressionists illustrates cross-cultural exchange in printmaking history (C.4) and non-Western painting traditions (B.6.b).

Holbein's definitive portraits of Henry VIII and court figures, along with The Ambassadors with its anamorphic skull, exemplify Northern Renaissance portrait painting (B.5.b.i) and the relationship between art and political power within Western painting history (B.6.a).

mappings.episodeTitle: Bruegel's The Fight Between Carnival and Lent
mappings.rationaleAI: Detailed analysis of Bruegel's 1559 painting addresses composition and design elements (B.1), iconographic interpretation of its rich symbolism (B.2), and genre painting depicting popular culture (B.5.b.i) within the Northern Renaissance tradition (B.6.a).

Delacroix's allegorical figure of Liberty addresses painting composition (B.1), the iconography of national symbolism (B.2), and narrative representational painting (B.5.b.i), situating the work within French Romantic painting history (B.6.a).

Guernica's Cubist and Surrealist monochrome techniques address painting composition (B.1), its anti-war symbolism constitutes modern iconography (B.2), and its abstracted forms bridge representational narrative painting (B.5.b.i) and nonrepresentational painting (B.5.b.ii) as a landmark of twentieth-century Western art (B.6.a).

Directly covers the emergence of photography as an art form and its relationship to painting (D.1), the development of daguerreotype and calotype equipment and chemical processes (D.2), and early portrait photography as a subject category (D.3.a).

mappings.episodeTitle: Songs of Innocence and of Experience
mappings.rationaleAI: Blake's unique relief etching technique for his hand-coloured illuminated books represents an innovative intaglio printmaking medium (C.2.b), constituting a significant development in the history of printmaking (C.4) that integrated visual art with text.

mappings.episodeTitle: Giorgio Vasari's Lives of the Artists
mappings.rationaleAI: Vasari's Lives chronicles Italian painting and drawing from Giotto through Michelangelo, establishing the progressive narrative and periodization that defined the history of Western painting (B.6.a) and drawing (A.4), and founding art history as a discipline applied to these visual arts.

The lavishly illuminated Lindisfarne Gospels exemplify manuscript illumination as a painting form on movable objects (B.5.a.ii), blending Celtic, Anglo-Saxon, and Mediterranean artistic traditions in early medieval insular art (B.6.b).

The golden ratio's recurrence in Renaissance art including Leonardo da Vinci's works directly relates to principles of composition and aesthetic proportion in painting (B.1) and drawing (A.1), connecting mathematical structure with visual design.

The vivid woodcut illustrations in Foxe's Actes and Monuments reached audiences beyond the literate elite, representing a significant use of relief printmaking (C.2.a) in the history of printmaking as a medium for mass visual communication during the Reformation (C.4).

Typological interpretation, reading Old Testament figures as prefigurations of Christ, directly shaped the iconography of medieval and Renaissance painting (B.2), determining how biblical subjects were depicted and paired across centuries of Western religious art.

The conflation of Mary Magdalene with the repentant sinner shaped painting iconography for two millennia (B.2), generating a major tradition of devotional and narrative representational painting (B.5.b.i) associating her with penitence and sexuality across Western art.

Hieronymus Bosch's elaborate painted visions of damnation represent a major tradition in painting iconography (B.2), with complex hierarchical depictions of hell constituting a significant subject in representational devotional painting (B.5.b.i).

Kahlo was a painter whose self-portraits and autobiographical canvases are central to the history of Western painting; her work in oil on canvas as representational painting (portraits, narrative painting) places her squarely within B.5.b.i, and as a major 20th-century painter within the history of painting (B.6.a).

Michelangelo's ceiling and altar wall are monumental fresco paintings (B.3 as painting media - fresco); they are fixed-object paintings as mural painting (B.5.a.i); the Genesis scenes and Last Judgement are devotional and narrative painting (B.5.b.i); the history of Western painting (B.6.a) - these represent a pinnacle of Renaissance painting
